title: Data centres can wind up energy
full_text:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen has bet that Australia’s booming data centre industry can reverse the slower than expected rollout of wind farms amid fears a slump in financing projects will result in Labor failing to hit its 2030 renewables target.
The capacity investment scheme devised by the Albanese government to supercharge renewables investment has struggled to deliver, with the level of green energy generation reaching financial close plu ing to a decade low, lifting pressure on Labor to achieve 82 per cent renewables by 2030.
Data centres could help wind farm projects get off the ground as part of a proposed edict for operators to underwrite new renewable power supply and pay their full share of new grid connectivity so costs are not passed to consumers or businesses.
“Those principles are that data centres should bring their own additional renewable energy, they need to bring new renewable energy to the grid, whic is important,” Mr Bowen said on Tuesday.
“It could be useful, for example, in helping some of the wind farms that are in development, with power purchase agreements to make those wind farms more viable and get them to a final investment decision.” Onshore wind financial commitments have plunged into a funk at a delicate time for both federal and state leaders, who have staked taxpayer funds to deliver a fast transition to green energy before ageing coal power stations reach their end of life.
The volume of wind farms reaching financial close in 2025 fell 59 per cent to 857 megawatts, from 2.2 gigawatts, against a commitmentsannual investment committments of 6-7GW of new wind and solar generation needed through to the end of the decade and beyond.
NSW Premier Chris Minns said on Tuesday the state must proceed with the switch to green energy despite headwinds and challenges as it develops its string of giant renewable energy zones.
“We’ve been upfront about the consequences and the challenges with this renewable energy rollout from the very first day in office,” Mr Minns said.
“We are on the clock when it comes to the renewable energy rollout. And if we delay it even more than it’s currently been delayed, I promise you, as high as energy prices are today, they’ll be higher again tomorrow.” Asked whether the country could achieve its 2030 target, Mr Bowen said he did not accept that the goal was “not achievable”.
“I also don’t think it’s inevitable,” he said. “I think we need to keep our foot on the accelerator and keep working.” Australia has a record 64GW of generation and storage waiting for grid connection, but the giant pipeline of projects is failing to develop into committed investments.
Rystad Energy said the wind sector was operating through one of its most challenging periods in recen memory, while stressing that the long-term fundamentals for the energy source in Australia remained strong.
“Across onshore and offshore wind, the industry is navigating: rising project costs, transmission bottlenecks, increasingly complex rid connection requirements, planning and permitting delays, community opposition and misinformation, supply chain and logistics constraints, and political uncertainty around renewable policy,” Rystad’s Gero Farruggio said.
Data centres are expected to more than double their power needs by 2030, driven by an AI boom, with Jim Chalmers betting on a bulging pipeline of data centre projects to carry business investment in the next few years.
AirTrunk estimates that for every additional gigawatt of data centre demand, Australia will probably need about 3-4 gigawatts of new renewable generation capacity to support 24/7 operations.
Mr Bowen did not accept there was a “choice” between limiting the growth of data centres and lowering power prices when asked about their demand on the grid, which is forecasted to jump in the coming years.
Major electricity transmission companies including Transgrid, Ausnet and Vicgrid warned that the rush to install major data centres has i reased security risks to the power grid as authorities scramble to limit the threat of blackouts.
Mr Bowen said rules for data centres could be “properly calibrated” to ensure the sector was a “net plus” for the energy grid.
But he added: “Without those rules and guidelines we put in place, it would be a challenge, but we’re avoiding that problem.” Data centres may place substantial pressure on water infrastructure with a need for guaranteed, uninterrupted supply posing security risks for customers under drought and climate change scenarios, NSW’s Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal also warned on Tuesday.
IPART is weighing whether its existing regulatory framework rem ns fit for purpose as the AI boom drives a surge in data centre factories, which rely on high water and renewable energy needs.
Water security risks may force utilities to accelerate or expand investment into desalination, increasing costs for consumers.
System-wide risks to water security may include “increased or volatile demand, including where demand from new developments causes an expansion or acceleration in investment into drought-resilient water supply sources, or where data centres close or change demand rapidly,” IPART said. It said data centre developers may opt out of determined prices and instead enter into bilateral negotiated services agreements.
